title: Chamomile
subtitle: Matricaria recutita — the kitchen's evening cup
excerpt: Single-herb profile. Botanical, traditional actions, key constituents, the western herbal canon's most-cited evening herb. The first materia-medica entry every reader should meet.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), chamomile entry — the western canonical reference, traditional uses across two centuries. Nicholas Culpeper, The English Physician (1652) — the seventeenth-century English record of chamomile in domestic medicine. King's American Dispensatory (Felter & Lloyd, 1898) — the Eclectic-American tradition's chamomile. EMA HMPC monograph on Matricaria flos (2015) — modern conservative summary including dosing and safety. WHO Monograph on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 1 (1999) — cross-tradition evidence summary.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb traditionally associated with calming or supporting the nervous system. Categorical, not a clinical claim.
A herb that eases trapped wind and digestive spasm, traditionally by relaxing the gut muscle.
The daisy plant family — chamomile, calendula, yarrow, echinacea, ragweed, chrysanthemum. Cross-reactive allergens.

content.text: Chamomile is the western kitchen's end-of-day cup, recorded in Maud Grieve's Modern Herbal (1931), Culpeper's English Physician (1652), and almost every English-language domestic medicine book before and between them. The plant is a small annual with feathery leaves and white-petalled flowers with a yellow centre. The flowers carry the action; the kitchen meets them as dried whole flowers in the tea cup.

attrs.title: Important safety notes
attrs.body:
Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms.

Chamomile is in the daisy family (Asteraceae), which means cross-reactive pollens with ragweed, chrysanthemum, calendula, and yarrow. Readers with a known daisy-family allergy should patch-test any chamomile preparation before wider use — dab a small amount on the inside of the forearm and wait 24-48 hours.

No clinically significant drug interactions are documented at culinary-tea doses. Concentrated chamomile tinctures (1-5 ml three times a day) may mildly potentiate sedatives and benzodiazepines; readers on prescription sedatives should consult their prescriber before starting therapeutic-dose preparations.

Pregnancy: culinary-strength chamomile tea is widely tolerated. Therapeutic-dose preparations (strong daily tinctures) are best discussed with a herbalist or midwife rather than self-prescribed.

content.text: Botanical
Matricaria recutita — German chamomile — is the annual the western herbal canon treats as the default. Family
Asteraceae

, height 20-50 cm, with a finely branched feathery foliage and 1-2 cm daisy-style flowers in mid to late summer. The pollen-bearing yellow centre is hollow when split open — the test that separates true chamomile from its lookalike scentless mayweed (Tripleurospermum inodorum), which has a solid centre and almost no scent. True chamomile smells distinctly of apple and honey when the flowers are crushed; the smell is the second confirmation.

Roman chamomile (Chamaemelum nobile) is the perennial sibling — a different species with a more bitter cup and a stronger ground-cover habit. Western herbal practice cites both; this profile follows Matricaria recutita, the annual that the EMA monograph and most modern open-access research uses as the reference plant.

Part used: the flowers — whole heads, dried in the dark to preserve the volatile oils. Harvesting in the garden takes the flowers in full open at midday on a dry day; commercial dried chamomile from a herbalist supplier is the kitchen norm.
Constituents and actions

The principal constituents are the volatile oil (with chamazulene, bisabolol, and bisabolol oxides), the flavonoids apigenin and luteolin, and a small set of coumarins. Chamazulene is the constituent that gives the steam-distilled chamomile essential oil its deep blue colour — it forms during the distillation rather than sitting in the dried flower as a blue compound.
Western herbal practice names four primary actions:
Chamomile is traditionally described as a
nervine

, taken in the evening to ease a wound-up day. The constituent apigenin is the one most often associated with this action in the modern open-access literature. Phrasing matters here: chamomile is not a sleeping pill and the publication does not present it as one.
It is also a
carminative

— the kitchen's after-meal cup for indigestion, mild bloating, and trapped wind. The volatile oil is the constituent traditionally credited with the digestive relaxation; the same action makes it the standard infant remedy in continental European tradition for the gentle settle after a meal (always at low strength, always cleared with the GP first for infants under two).

Anti-inflammatory action — bisabolol and the flavonoids — is the constituent set that makes chamomile a traditional external compress for mild skin irritation, sore eyes (cool compress over closed eyelids), and inflamed gums (cooled-infusion mouth rinse, never with infants' teething where the rinse can be aspirated).

And a mild sedative — the action that makes a strong tincture (1-5 ml three times a day) a herbalist's choice for short-term sleep support. The publication's voice doesn't claim sedation from a culinary cup; the strong-tincture dose is the one the tradition uses for that action.
Traditional uses

Western herbal: Grieve records chamomile as "a great preserver of horses' health" alongside its human uses — the herb has been a working-stable plant in European traditions for as long as it has been a kitchen one. The cup after the evening meal, the cooled compress over the sore eye, the warm bath for restless children with growing pains. Culpeper writes of it as a herb "for the breast" — meaning the lungs — drunk hot in the evening through cold-season weeks.

Folk European: chamomile is the German Heilpflanze des Jahres (medicinal plant of the year) of 1987 — a recognition by the German herbal community of its place in continental domestic medicine. The Manzanilla of Spain — "little apple" — names the herb for its apple-like smell. The same name appears across Iberia and Latin America for the same use.

Eclectic-American: Felter and Lloyd's King's American Dispensatory (1898) records the same primary uses with one notable addition — chamomile as a cooled-infusion eye-wash for mild conjunctivitis, applied with cotton wool dabbed in the cooled tea. The eye use is a kitchen tradition the publication mentions with a careful caveat: see your GP for any eye problem that doesn't clear in 24 hours, and never apply herbal preparations to an injured eye.
Preparations

Hot infusion is the kitchen default — 1-2 teaspoons of dried flowers in a covered cup, just-off-boil water, steeped 8-10 minutes. One cup in the evening, or after a meal that needs settling. The lid traps the volatile oils that carry the action; without the lid, the cup is half a remedy.

Strong infusion (1:10, double the standard ratio) is the base for an external compress, cooled to skin-tolerable, applied with a clean cloth over the area for 10-20 minutes. The same strong infusion, further diluted with water, is the mouth-rinse for inflamed gums.

Tincture (1:5 dried-herb-to-40%-spirit by weight, macerated 4-6 weeks) is the herbalist's preparation for the stronger sedative action and the most reliable long-term storage form. Dose 1-5 ml three times a day at therapeutic strength. Tinctures are not the publication's first recommendation for a beginner — the cup is. The tincture is the next step for readers who want a more concentrated form, and the entry-level case where the publication would suggest seeing a herbalist before increasing the strength on your own.

Bath: 50 g of dried flowers steeped 30 minutes in 1 litre of just-boiled water, strained, added to a warm bath. The traditional preparation for restless evenings and growing-pains aches in older children. Not for infants in any form.
Cautions and contraindications

Allergy: Asteraceae cross-reactivity. Readers with a known ragweed, daisy, chrysanthemum, or calendula allergy should patch-test before any new chamomile preparation. Test: dab a small amount on the inside of the forearm; wait 24-48 hours; look for redness, itch, or swelling. Reactions to chamomile are not common but they happen, and they're easier to head off in a patch-test than after a steaming cup.

Pregnancy and breastfeeding: culinary-strength tea (one cup of standard 1-2 teaspoon-per-cup chamomile) is widely tolerated. Therapeutic-dose preparations (strong tinctures, daily multiple cups of double-strength tea) are best discussed with a herbalist or your midwife rather than self-prescribed. There's no evidence of a problem with the cup; there's also no body of evidence supporting therapeutic-dose use as actively beneficial in pregnancy, which is the publication's threshold for recommending therapeutic doses.

Children: standard-strength tea (1 teaspoon per cup, steeped 5 minutes) is widely tolerated from school age, one cup in the evening. For infants and toddlers, consult a paediatric herbalist or your GP — adult-divided-by-half is not safe paediatric dosing. The continental European tradition uses very dilute warm chamomile for infant settle after feeds, but that practice is herbalist-supervised in the tradition that records it, not a home recommendation.

Drug interactions: no clinically significant interactions documented at culinary doses. Therapeutic-dose tincture may mildly potentiate prescription sedatives, benzodiazepines, and CNS depressants — consult your prescriber before starting a daily tincture if you take any of these. Chamomile has been studied for mild anticoagulant activity in vitro; the clinical picture is unclear, but readers on warfarin or anti-platelet drugs should mention chamomile-tincture use to their prescriber.

When to skip and see a doctor: any allergic reaction at patch-test or after first cup (swelling around mouth, hives, breathing changes) — call 111 or 999 for any breathing change. Any sleep problem that lasts more than a month, comes with low mood, snoring with daytime sleepiness, or any sudden change in sleep pattern in an older adult — see your GP rather than reach for the cup.
